title: What is a Video?
concept: A video is a fast sequence of static images (frames) shown one after another to create the illusion of motion.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main goal of computer vision when working with static images?

options.text: To classify, locate, or segment objects in a single snapshot
To convert images into audio files
To edit and paint colors on photos

exercises.explain: Correct! Image-based vision looks at individual snapshots to understand 'what' and 'where' things are.

cards.title: The flipbook trick
cards.body: Remember drawing a stick figure on the corners of a notebook and flipping the pages fast to make it run? That flipbook is exactly how movies and digital videos work! Your eyes are fooled into seeing motion by a series of still pictures.

cards.title: Frames and Video
cards.body: A video is a sequence of static images, called frames, captured and displayed at a constant rate. Instead of a single standalone image, video data is a collection of ordered images with a strict timeline.

cards.title: A stack of polaroids
cards.body: Imagine taking a photo of a running dog every second and stacking them in order. Each individual photo is a frame. If you look at them one by one, you see static poses. If you shuffle through them quickly, you see the dog run!

cards.title: Video file structure
cards.body: When a computer opens a video file, it reads a series of pixel arrays. A 5-second video shot at 30 frames per second contains 150 individual images that play in a specific, timed order.

cards.title: Not a continuous stream
cards.body: You might think video is a continuous, fluid flow of light. In reality, computers only see separate, distinct snapshots. The illusion of smoothness is created entirely by our brain's persistence of vision!

cards.title: Image vs. Video Data
cards.body: Comparing the structure of a single image versus a video:

left.text:
One static snapshot
Dimensions: Height x Width x Channels
No concept of time

right.text:
Sequence of N snapshots
Dimensions: Frames x Height x Width x Channels
Adds the time dimension

exercises.prompt: What are the individual static images in a video sequence called?
exercises.explain: Correct! Individual images that make up a video are called frames.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A computer stores a video as a continuous fluid stream rather than a series of individual images.
exercises.explain: False. Computers store and process video as a discrete sequence of static image frames.

exercises.sentence: A single image has spatial dimensions, whereas a video adds the ___ dimension.
exercises.explain: The temporal (time) dimension represents the progression of frames over time.

exercises.prompt: Which of these datasets represents video data?

options.text: A folder containing 500 photos of cats
A sequence of 120 ordered frames showing a person jumping

exercises.explain: A sequence of ordered frames captured over time is video data.

exercises.prompt: Why does a video model need to know the order of the frames to understand actions like 'standing up' vs. 'sitting down'?

options.text: Because the same set of frames can mean opposite actions if played forward vs. backward.
Because frame ordering increases the resolution of each individual image.
Because backwards videos run slower on modern GPUs.
Because frame ordering changes the color space of the video.

exercises.explain: Exactly! The temporal order of frames defines the action. Reversing the sequence changes the meaning completely (e.g., sitting down vs. standing up). Next, let's look at how fast these frames must play!
